metadata.title: Promote, Reflect & Ship
metadata.playlist: Build a Home — Thought Leadership on Substack
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 10 brief — Self-Promotion Through Thought Leadership

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 5 of 5. Publishing is half the job. The second half is promoting it so it reaches the people it is for, reflecting on the strategic choices you made so the grader knows they were deliberate, and assembling everything into the submission PDF. Three deliverables in this episode: the LinkedIn promotion post, the three-hundred-word strategic reflection, and the PDF. Let's close the assignment.

props.command: LinkedIn promo post (value-first, skep:preview) + 300-word strategic reflection + PDF assembled and submitted.
props.runningText: publishing is half the job — promotion reaches the people it's for, reflection proves it was deliberate…
output: LinkedIn promotion post (2–4 sentences, value-first hook, screenshot with engagement)
Submitted PDF with all required elements

beats.narration_text: The LinkedIn promotion post. Two to four sentences, value-first. Not 'check out my article' — a post that gives the reader something even if they never click through. The best promotion posts for thought-leadership content do one of two things. They give the sharpest line from the article — the hook sentence or the counterintuitive claim — and let it stand on its own, then point to the full piece for more. 'Most AI pipelines fail because the quality gate checks whether the pipeline ran, not whether the output was correct. Those are two different things — and most people find out the difference the hard way. Full piece in the comments.' Or they give the reader one actionable thing from the article's framework — a question to check their own work, a specific thing to look for — and point to the full piece for the rest. 'If you have an AI pipeline running right now, here is one question to check: does your quality gate verify that the output is meaningful, or just that a value was returned? Full article at the link below.' The skep:preview skill generates value-first captions from the article's sharpest line. After you post, take a screenshot of the LinkedIn post that shows any engagement — likes, comments, shares. If it was just posted, take the screenshot immediately with zero engagement and note the post date. That screenshot goes in the PDF.

artifactLines: 2–4 sentences. Value-first = reader gets something even if they don't click.
Type 1: sharpest line from article + 'full piece in the comments/link below.'
Type 2: one actionable thing from the framework + 'full article at the link.'
skep:preview → value-first caption from article's sharpest line.
Screenshot the post after publishing (even 0 engagement is fine). Goes in the PDF.
props.sparkLine: Value-first: the reader gets something without clicking. Then they click.

beats.narration_text: The strategic reflection. Three hundred words, and this is worth twenty points — it is the proof that you were thinking strategically, not just writing. A mechanical reflection answers the questions. A strategic reflection shows a mind working through what it learned. The reflection covers three things. First: the strategic choices. Why this angle, why this structure, why this tone. Not 'I chose the Hero's Journey because it fit my story' — 'I chose the problem-as-user-story angle because my thesis is about a failure that happens at scale, and a personal narrative would have made it seem like a one-off incident rather than a structural issue.' Name the choices and the reasoning. Second: how this positions you differently. In the landscape from Assignment 6's competitive matrix — how does a practitioner who publishes thought leadership on AI pipeline quality gate design sit differently than one who does not? What does this article establish that the LinkedIn profile alone could not? Third: what you would do differently, and what the next piece is. The 'differently' is honest learning — at least one thing you would change and why. The 'next piece' is one sentence naming the follow-on topic: 'The next article will go deeper on the IJ gate — Ingest Judgment — because the pattern I described in this piece has a specific variant at the raw-to-verified boundary that deserves its own treatment.' That one sentence signals to the grader that this is not a one-time submission — it is the first issue of a real publication.

artifactLines: Part 1 — Choices: why this angle, structure, tone. Name the reasoning, not just the choice.
Part 2 — Positioning: how does publishing this sit you differently in A6's competitive landscape?
What does this establish that a LinkedIn profile alone cannot?
Part 3 — Next steps: one honest thing you'd change + one sentence naming the next article topic.
'Next piece' signals this is a real publication, not a one-time submission.
props.sparkLine: Name the reasoning. Signal the next piece. That's strategic — not mechanical.

beats.act: pdf assembly and the complete submission checklist
beats.narration_text: The PDF. Assemble everything into a single PDF. Five required elements in order. One: the Substack publication link — clickable, showing the publication name and tagline. Two: the article link — the full URL of the live article, clickable, and a screenshot of the published article showing the headline, the visual, and the first paragraph. Three: the brand audit from Episode 2 — the three screenshots side by side and the coherence explanation. Four: the LinkedIn post screenshot — the actual post with any engagement, or zero engagement with the post date visible. Five: the three-hundred-word reflection. Name the file LastName_FirstName_Assignment10.pdf. Submit to Canvas. Before you export, run the final checklist. Substack link clickable and publicly accessible? Article link clickable and publicly accessible? Brand audit screenshots match the actual current state of the three surfaces? LinkedIn screenshot from after you posted, not a draft? Reflection is three hundred words and names specific choices, not generic statements? File named correctly and under any size limit? And the final voice check: read one paragraph of the reflection out loud. If it sounds like a template, rewrite it. The assignment is about self-promotion through thought leadership — the reflection that sounds like it was generated by a prompt is the one that fails to demonstrate that you were actually thinking.

artifactLines: 1. Substack publication link — clickable, shows name + tagline.
2. Article link — clickable URL + screenshot (headline + visual + first paragraph).
3. Brand audit (E2) — 3 screenshots + coherence explanation.
4. LinkedIn post screenshot — after posting, engagement or date visible.
5. 300-word reflection — specific choices, not generic statements.
Final voice check: read 1 paragraph of reflection aloud. Template language = rewrite.
props.sparkLine: 5 elements. Clickable links. Reflection that sounds like you thought about it.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Promote, Reflect, and Ship. LinkedIn promotion post: two to four sentences, value-first — the reader gets something even if they do not click. Screenshot with engagement or post date. Three-hundred-word strategic reflection: why this angle and structure, how this positions you differently in the competitive landscape, what you would change, and the one sentence naming the next article. The reflection that sounds like a template fails the assignment's purpose. PDF: five required elements in order — Substack link, article link plus screenshot, brand audit, LinkedIn screenshot, reflection. File named correctly. Links clickable and publicly accessible. That is Assignment 10.

artifactLines: LinkedIn promo: 2–4 sentences, value-first (reader gains something without clicking). Screenshot.
Reflection (300 words): angle/structure reasoning · competitive positioning · what you'd change · next piece.
Reflection that sounds like a template fails the assignment's purpose.
PDF: 5 elements — Substack link · article link + screenshot · brand audit · LinkedIn screenshot · reflection.
Links clickable + publicly accessible. File: LastName_FirstName_Assignment10.pdf.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. The promotion post prompt, then the reflection prompt. First, the promotion post — paste this into Claude or skep:preview. The prompt reads: I published a thought-leadership article at this URL: [paste URL]. The sharpest line in the article — the hook or the counterintuitive claim — is: [paste it]. The one actionable thing from the article's framework that the reader can apply today is: [describe it in one sentence]. Write two versions of a LinkedIn promotion caption: Version 1 uses the sharpest line as the hook and points to the full article. Version 2 gives the actionable thing and points to the full article. Both versions: two to four sentences, value-first, no 'excited to share' or 'I'm proud to announce,' end with 'link in comments.' Pick the version that sounds more like a real person describing something useful. Second, the reflection prompt: I am writing a three-hundred-word strategic reflection on my Assignment 10 article. My angle was [angle], my structure was [Path A or B], and my tone was [describe in one to two words]. Explain why I made those choices in terms of the argument I was trying to make and the reader I was writing for. Then describe how publishing this piece sits me differently in my competitive landscape from Assignment 6 — name the specific white space from the positioning matrix. Then name one thing I would do differently and one sentence on the next article topic. Write in the first person, direct voice, three hundred words. The reflection must sound like a person who actually thought about the choices — not a summary of the assignment brief.

props.command: Promo: URL [paste] + sharpest line [paste] + 1 actionable thing [describe]. Write 2 captions (sharpest-line version + actionable version). Pick the one that sounds like a real person. Reflection: angle [name] + Path [A/B] + tone [1–2 words] → 300 words: why those choices · competitive white space from A6 · what you'd change · next article sentence.
props.runningText: reflection must sound like someone who thought about the choices — not a summary of the brief…

beats.narration_text: Promote, Reflect & Ship. Liam, in for Bear.
